lesson.name: Game and Graphics Programming - Lesson 3 — NES Programming

messages.content:
### Lesson Summary: NES Architecture and Programming

This lesson provides an introduction to the hardware specifications and memory structure of the Nintendo Entertainment System (NES), essential for developing retro-style games using the 6502 assembly language.

#### **1. Core Hardware Specifications**
*   **CPU:** Ricoh 2A03 (a version of the MOS 6502).
*   **Video (PPU):** Ricoh 2C02 with a resolution of 256x240 pixels. It supports 4 background palettes and 4 sprite palettes, rendering tiles (8x8) and sprites (8x8 or 8x16).
*   **Sound:** Features 5 channels: 2 Pulse waves, 1 Triangle wave, 1 Noise channel, and 1 DPCM channel.

#### **2. Memory Map**
The NES utilizes a specific memory layout:
*   **Internal RAM:** Allocated to Zero Page ($0000–$00FF), Stack memory ($0100–$01FF), and general RAM ($0200–$07FF).
*   **Mirroring:** Addresses from $0800 to $1FFF mirror the internal RAM/Stack/Zero Page.
*   **Storage & I/O:** Includes I/O registers ($2000–$2007, $4000–$401F), Expansion ROM ($4020–$5FFF), Save RAM ($6000–$7FFF), and PRG-ROM ($8000–$FFFF).
*   **Interrupt Vectors:** The final bytes of memory ($FFFA–$FFFF) store addresses for NMI, Reset, and Break handler routines.

#### **3. Input/Output (I/O) Control**
*   **PPU Registers ($2000–$2007):** Used to control video settings, scrolling, and sprite/memory addressing.
*   **Sound Registers ($4000–$4015):** Controls volume, wave patterns, frequencies, and channel enabling.
*   **Joysticks ($4016–$4017):** Dedicated ports for controller input.

#### **4. Development Resources**
For further study, the documents recommend:
*   **NesDev (nesdev.com):** A hub for technical documentation and community tools.
*   **P65 Assembler:** A Perl-based tool useful for 6502 architecture development.

***

**References:** [S.1], [S.2], [S.3], [S.4], [S.5]

Lesson 3 — NES Programming - Presentation6 slides
NES Programming Fundamentals
  • Introduction to 6502-based retro game development.
  • Overview of Ricoh 2A03 CPU and system specs.

### Summary of Nintendo Entertainment System (NES) Hardware

The NES is powered by the Ricoh 2A03 CPU, which is custom-configured based on the MOS 6502 architecture with the decimal mode removed. It operates at different clock speeds depending on the region:

*   **NTSC:** $1.789773 \text{ MHz}$
*   **PAL:** $1.773447 \text{ MHz}$

**Memory and Display**
The system includes $2 \text{ KB}$ of internal memory and utilizes the Ricoh 2C02 Picture Processing Unit (PPU). Key visual specifications include:
*   **Resolution:** A base resolution of $256 \times 240$ pixels, though only $256 \times 224$ pixels are visible in NTSC mode.
*   **Color Palette:** A fixed set of 52 colors.
*   **Graphics Handling:** Backgrounds are composed of $8 \times 8$ pixel tiles. The system supports up to 64 sprites (either $8 \times 8$ or $8 \times 16$ pixels), with a hardware constraint of 8 sprites per scan-line.

**Audio Capabilities**
The sound hardware features five distinct channels:
*   2 Pulse wave channels
*   1 Triangle wave channel
*   1 Noise channel
*   1 DPCM channel

### NES Memory Map Summary

The memory architecture of the NES is organized into specific ranges, each serving dedicated functions for system operation, storage, and communication.

**Primary Memory Regions**
*   **Zero Page ($0000–$00FF):** Optimized memory providing faster access speeds for read and write operations.
*   **Stack Memory ($0100–$01FF):** Reserved space for stack operations.
*   **General RAM ($0200–$07FF):** Standard system RAM consisting of 1536 bytes.
*   **Mirrored Memory ($0800–$1FFF):** Multiple ranges that mirror the primary Zero Page, Stack, and RAM regions.
*   **Save RAM ($6000–$7FFF):** 8192 bytes of SRAM used for persistent data storage between sessions.
*   **PRG-ROM ($8000–$FFFF):** The largest block of memory (32768 bytes), containing the game program code.

**Input/Output and Expansion**
*   **I/O Registers:** Located at $2000–$2007 and $4000–$401F, used for interfacing with hardware. The former is echoed multiple times throughout the $2008–$3FFF range.
*   **Expansion ROM ($4020–$5FFF):** Reserved for custom hardware features, specifically used by the MMC5 mapper to enhance VRAM capabilities.

**Interrupt Vectors**
The final bytes of the addressable space are reserved for critical system handler addresses:
*   NMI Handler: $FFFA–$FFFB
*   Power-on Reset Handler: $FFFC–$FFFD
*   Break (BRK) Handler: $FFFE–$FFFF

This section outlines the hardware input/output (I/O) memory map for the NES, specifically detailing addresses related to the Picture Processing Unit (PPU) and the Square Wave sound channels.

The PPU I/O ports reside in the address range $\$2000-\$2007$:

$\$2000$: Write-only PPU Control Register 1.
$\$2001$: Write-only PPU Control Register 2.
$\$2002$: Read-only PPU Status Register.
$\$2003$: Write-only Sprite Memory Address.
$\$2004$: Read/Write Sprite Memory Data.
$\$2005$: Write-only Background Scroll register.
$\$2006$: Write-only PPU Memory Address for indexing.
$\$2007$: Read/Write PPU Memory Data for data access.

The Square Wave 1 sound channel occupies addresses $\$4000-\$4003$:

$\$4000$: Controls volume, envelope decay, looping, and duty cycle.
$\$4001$: Configures the frequency sweep parameters.
$\$4002$: Defines the lower 8 bits of the wavelength.
$\$4003$: Defines the upper 3 bits of the wavelength and the length counter load.

This document outlines the memory-mapped register configuration for audio channels and direct memory access on the NES/Famicom system.

**Audio Channel Registers**

- **Triangle Channel ($4008–$400B):** Register 1 ($4008) controls the linear counter and length counter clock disable. Registers 3 and 4 operate similarly to Square Wave 1.
- **Noise Channel ($400C–$400F):** Register 3 ($400E) manages the playback sample rate and random number generation type. Registers 1 and 4 match Square Wave 1.
- **DMC Channel ($4010–$4013):** Register 1 ($4010) sets the sample rate. Register 2 ($4011) controls volume. Register 3 ($4012) determines the voice table start address at $N \times 0x40 + 0xc000$. Register 4 ($4013) defines the voice table size as $N \times 16 + 1$.

**System Control and Access**

- **Sprite Memory:** Register $4014$ is a write-only register dedicated to DMA access for sprite memory.
- **Sound Enable:** Register $4015$ is a read/write register used to toggle individual sound channels on or off.

### Summary of NES Programming Resources

This section provides technical guidance for Super NES development, focusing on hardware interaction and external community resources.

**Key Technical Details**
* Memory addresses $4016 and $4017 are designated for reading and writing data to Joystick 1 and Joystick 2, respectively.

**Recommended Development Resources**
* **Community Support:** The #nesdev channel on the EFnet IRC server is the primary hub for developer discussions.
* **Documentation and Tools:** NesDev (nesdev.com) serves as the central repository for technical documentation, software tools, and code examples.
* **Assembly Programming:** The P65 Assembler, a Perl-based tool, is available for developing software specifically for the 6502 processor family used in the NES.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why is the use of the 'Zero Page' ($0000–$00FF) significant for a developer programming on the Ricoh 2A03 CPU?
options: It is the only memory safe from being mirrored at higher addresses.
It allows for faster memory read/write access using special addressing modes.
It is the only area of memory that persists after the console is powered off.
It provides the necessary extra bandwidth for DPCM sound processing.
questions.explanation: The Zero Page is optimized for faster access in the 6502 architecture, making it the most efficient location for frequently accessed variables.

questions.question: If a developer wants to update the graphics on the screen, why must they use registers $2006 and $2007?
options: Because the PPU cannot access main system RAM directly.
Because $2006 is used to set the memory address inside the PPU, and $2007 is used to actually transfer the data.
Because the system requires two-step verification for all video memory writes to prevent bugs.
Because $2005 is strictly reserved for sprite coordinates and cannot handle background tiles.
questions.explanation: $2006 is the PPU address register used to point to a specific location in PPU memory, and $2007 is the data register through which the data is written to or read from that location.

questions.question: The CPU memory map shows several address ranges marked as 'Mirror of $0000–$07FF'. What does this imply for the programmer?
options: Writing to $0800 automatically updates the data at $0000.
The hardware has 8KB of dedicated RAM separate from the Zero Page.
The mirror addresses are faster than the original addresses and should be preferred.
The mirror addresses are read-only to prevent accidental data corruption.
questions.explanation: Because these addresses map to the same physical memory, writing to the mirror addresses ($0800, etc.) changes the same bytes as writing to the original range ($0000-$07FF).

questions.question: You are designing a scene with many moving entities. Given the hardware limitations of the NES, what is the constraint on sprites?
options: You can have 64 sprites active in total, but only 8 can be displayed on any single scan-line.
You are limited to exactly 8 sprites total on the entire screen at once.
Sprites must be 8x16 pixels; 8x8 pixels is not supported by the hardware.
Only 64 sprites can be displayed per scan-line, but there is no limit on total sprites.
questions.explanation: The NES supports up to 64 sprites in memory, but the PPU is hardware-limited to displaying only 8 sprites per scan-line.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the memory addresses $FFFA through $FFFF?
options: These are general-purpose RAM addresses for storing game score data.
They act as the primary I/O ports for the two joystick controllers.
They store the entry point addresses for specific system vectors like NMI, Reset, and BRK handlers.
They are used to communicate directly with the Ricoh 2A03's audio registers.
questions.explanation: These addresses (vectors) tell the CPU which memory locations to jump to when specific system events occur, such as a hardware reset or an interrupt.

cards.front: What is the CPU of the NES and how does it differ from a standard MOS 6502?
cards.back: The NES uses a Ricoh 2A03 CPU, which is essentially a MOS 6502 processor with its decimal mode removed.

cards.front: Which NES memory range provides faster read/write access?
What are the addresses for the NMI, Power-on Reset, and BRK interrupt handler routines?
What is the PPU resolution of the NES?
cards.back: 256 x 240 pixels (only 256 x 224 pixels are visible on NTSC).

cards.front: How many sprites can the NES display per scan-line?
What are the sound channel components of the NES?
cards.back: 2 pulse wave channels, 1 triangle wave channel, 1 noise channel, and 1 DPCM channel.

cards.front: Which I/O register address is used for DMA access to sprite memory?
What memory range is dedicated to PRG-ROM?
Which I/O register address allows reading from the joystick?
What is the purpose of the $2006 register?
cards.back: $2006 is a write-only PPU Memory Address register used for indexing into PPU memory locations.

slides.title: NES Programming Fundamentals
bullets: Overview of Ricoh 2A03 CPU and system specs.
slides.notes: Welcome to our third lesson on NES programming. We will start by exploring the core hardware architecture that powers these classic games.

slides.title: NES Hardware Specifications
bullets: Ricoh 2A03 CPU with disabled decimal mode.
2 KB internal RAM and custom PPU graphics.
slides.notes: The Ricoh 2A03 is our engine, restricted to integer arithmetic. We also have a PPU for graphics and four distinct sound channels.

slides.title: Memory Map Overview
bullets: $0000–$01FF hosts Zero Page and Stack RAM.
slides.notes: Understanding the memory map is crucial, as mapping your code and data correctly determines how the CPU interacts with the PPU and inputs.

slides.title: PPU Video Control
bullets: $2000–$2001 set PPU control and rendering modes.
$2002 monitors PPU status via read access.
$2006–$2007 facilitate PPU memory addressing and I/O.
slides.notes: The PPU is controlled through specific I/O registers. These allow you to push graphics data and manipulate the screen state.

slides.title: Sound and Advanced I/O
slides.notes: Beyond visuals, the register set handles audio synthesis and controller polling, enabling the interactive nature of NES games.

slides.title: Development Resources
bullets: Join the #nesdev IRC community for support.
External tools bridge modern code to hardware.
slides.notes: Wrap up your learning by leveraging these community resources and specialized assemblers for your development workflow.
